justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Breaking change clearly identified — called out as 'fundamental structural change to a core aggregate boundary' with 1:1→1:N cardinality change. (2) New minimum-cardinality invariant explicitly stated in BR-1 with testable assertion. (3) Data migration impact for 2M existing records covered in BR-4, edge cases (dirty data), and business_impact.operational. (4) External API breakage identified in BR-5, AC-4/AC-5, and a dedicated escalation with backward-compat options. (5) 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as a dedicated escalation and in low_confidence_areas. (6) Architectural concerns extensively flagged via escalations covering status model, billing, API versioning, and data migration strategy — though MJ is not named explicitly, the escalations clearly call for architectural decisions. (7) confidence.level=52, well within <=75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough business rules — 7 rules with invariant classifications, justifications, and testable assertions far exceed expectations
Edge cases are outstanding: dirty migration data, same-address recipients, per-recipient cancellation, analytics query breakage — these go well beyond the rubric
Four well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, covering status model, billing, API compat, and delivery preferences
Stakeholder analysis identifies 7 distinct groups with specific impacts — courier workflow changes and customer support training are particularly insightful
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the number of genuinely missing business decisions identified
Existing invariant change clearly noted — 'A Shipment has exactly one Recipient' is implicitly replaced by the new BR-1 minimum-one constraint
No explicit escalation to MJ for architectural migration plan — escalations are all typed 'ambiguity' rather than flagging one as needing MJ's architectural input
Acceptance criteria are concrete and testable, including backward-compat scenarios (AC-3, AC-4, AC-5) and the zero-recipient guard (AC-7)

trials.justification: All pass criteria met. Breaking change clearly identified ('fundamental structural change to a core aggregate'). New minimum-cardinality invariant explicitly stated in BR-1 ('A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times'). Data migration impact for 2M records noted in BR-5, business_impact.operational, and confidence.high_confidence_areas. External API breakage identified in BR-6, AC-4, edge_cases, and a dedicated escalation on backward compatibility strategy. 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as the first escalation with concrete options. Architectural concerns flagged extensively across escalations (API versioning strategy, aggregate boundary questions, migration planning) though MJ is not named explicitly — the architectural migration need is clearly communicated. Confidence at 72, well within the <=75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough business rules — 7 rules with invariant justifications and testable assertions, going well beyond the minimum required
Correctly identifies the implicit invariant change: 'A Shipment has exactly one Recipient' becoming 'at least one Recipient' (BR-1)
Data migration risk for 2M records addressed with semantic preservation requirement (BR-5), not just mechanical wrapping
External API breakage covered from multiple angles: partner contract preservation (BR-6), backward-compatible responses (AC-4), and a dedicated escalation with three concrete options
Surfaces delivery preferences ambiguity with actionable options and a pragmatic recommendation
Raises a second high-value ambiguity not in the rubric: multi-drop vs multi-package semantics, which is a genuine domain question
Edge cases are strong — billing implications, concurrent Recipient updates, partial delivery failure, and clone semantics are all real operational concerns
Confidence at 72 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas that map to genuine unknowns
Does not explicitly name MJ or request architectural escalation by agent name — architectural concerns are raised but as ambiguity escalations rather than a direct handoff
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive — identifies 7 affected groups with specific impacts, including non-obvious ones like customer support tooling

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship, enabling multi-recipient shipments. This is a fundamental structural change to a core aggregate that impacts data models, external integrations, billing, routing, and operational workflows.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management — the core context responsible for shipment lifecycle, recipient information, and delivery coordination.","ubiq

trials.justification: All pass criteria met. Breaking change clearly identified as 1:1→1:N cardinality shift. Minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1). Data migration impact for ~2M records addressed (BR-5, edge cases). External API breakage identified with backward-compatibility strategy (BR-6, AC-3). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as first escalation with concrete options. Architectural concerns flagged across multiple escalations (physical fulfillment model, lifecycle constraints). Confidence at 52, well within ≤75 threshold. MJ not called out by name but architectural migration concerns are substantively present across escalations.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — identified breaking change, stated both the removed invariant (exactly one recipient) and the new minimum-cardinality invariant (at least one)
Data migration handled carefully in BR-5 with explicit testable assertion that existing records remain semantically unchanged
External API breakage addressed with concrete backward-compatibility strategy (BR-6, AC-3) including partner opt-in versioning
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced with three concrete resolution options and a practical recommendation
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the multiple undefined areas — low_confidence_areas list is specific and actionable
Went beyond rubric requirements: identified Primary Recipient concept for backward compatibility, partially-delivered composite status, pricing model gap, and physical fulfillment model ambiguity
Edge cases are excellent — multi-stop billing, in-transit recipient modification, partial delivery status, and legacy webhook serialization
No explicit escalation to MJ for architectural migration plan — escalations are all typed as 'ambiguity' rather than flagging one as needing MJ's architectural input
Business impact section is comprehensive — covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with six distinct stakeholder groups identified
